steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to support Browner's claim that Ballard's compositions rely on elements from Native musical traditions. A correct answer must do two things: (1) attribute the claim to Browner, and (2) provide a concrete piece of evidence from the notes that backs it up.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Classical composer Louis Ballard, who wrote Cacéga Ayuwípi, was nominated for the Pulitzer Prize in music in 1971.': No mention of Browner's claim at all, and the Pulitzer nomination is linked to Desert Trilogy, not Cacéga Ayuwípi — factual error. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Browner claims that Louis Ballard's compositions, one of which was nominated for the Pulitzer Prize, rely on elements originating within Native musical traditions.': Attributes the claim to Browner, but cites the Pulitzer nomination as evidence — that shows recognition, not reliance on Native traditions. No supporting evidence for the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — 'According to Browner, composer Louis Ballard, who wrote Desert Trilogy, relies on elements with origins in Native musical traditions.': Attributes the claim to Browner, but uses Desert Trilogy as the example. The notes give no evidence that Desert Trilogy incorporates Native elements — only Cacéga Ayuwípi does. No real evidence provided. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Browner posits that Louis Ballard's compositions rely on elements originating within Native musical traditions; Cacéga Ayuwípi affirms this assertion, incorporating Hopi notched sticks.': Attributes the claim to Browner, then cites the specific evidence from the notes — Cacéga Ayuwípi's use of Hopi notched sticks, a traditional Native instrument — to support it. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
